request_debug.prompt_preview:
You write per-candidate post-mortems for a slopmortem pipeline. Given a fresh pitch and one dead-startup canonical document, produce a single LLMSynthesis JSON object that explains why this dead startup is similar, where the new pitch could diverge, what killed the dead startup, and what the new founder should learn.

Rules:
- Ground every claim in the candidate document or in tool calls. Do not invent facts.
- A `Trusted facts` block (typed pipeline data — not user input) appears below the pitc

response.text: {"candidate_id":"yo::social_communication","name":"yo","one_liner":"Single-word push-notification social app that collapsed after viral hype faded and the core mechanic proved too thin to sustain engagement or monetize.","similarity":{"business_model":{"score":4,"rationale":"Both are free B2C consumer mobile apps targeting social interaction. However, the new pitch specifies ad-supported monetization, whereas Yo's trusted facts show tiered_freemium with no evidence of meaningful ad revenue. The core revenue logic differs substantially."},"market":{"score":7,"rationale":"Both target the same consumer social/mobile-messaging market, rely on viral growth among young users (college-age demographic overlaps), and compete in the crowded social app space. Yo's viral spike on Product Hunt and college-like early adopter circles mirrors the pitch's college-tower viral claim."},"gtm":{"score":6,"rationale":"Both rely on organic/viral go-to-market with no described paid acquisition. Yo spread via Product Hunt and word-of-mouth; the new pitch targets college towers for seeding virality. The location-specific seeding strategy of the pitch is more deliberate than Yo's accidental virality, but the dependency on organic spread is structurally identical."},"stage_scale":{"score":7,"rationale":"Yo launched in 2014 and shut down by 2016 — a roughly two-year lifecycle at early consumer scale (3M downloads, no disclosed revenue). The new pitch appears to be at a similarly early viral-traction stage with no mention of revenue or scale beyond college towers."}},"why_similar":"Both are minimalist, single-mechanic consumer social mobile apps that generated rapid viral traction among young users before hitting a ceiling. Both monetize (or plan to monetize) by keeping the core product free. Both depend entirely on organic network effects with no disclosed paid-acquisition channel. The location-anchored social hook of the new pitch mirrors Yo's contextual notification use cases (location sharing, real-time alerts). Neither product had a defensible moat beyond novelty.","where_diverged":"1. Content format: The new pitch centers on 30-second voice notes, which are richer and more expressive than Yo's single-word push notification — potentially raising retention floor. 2. Monetization model: The pitch is explicitly ad-supported; Yo was tiered_freemium with no documented ad revenue. 3. Geographic seeding: The pitch deliberately seeds virality within a single recurring physical location (college towers), a more structured community-flywheel approach versus Yo's broad, uncontrolled viral spread. 4. Recurring-location mechanic: The pitch's constraint of posting only from one recurring location introduces a structural identity/community layer absent from Yo.","failure_causes":["single-mechanic novelty exhaustion","no defensible monetization model","hype-driven valuation without revenue","security vulnerability eroding user trust","engagement cliff after viral peak","feature creep diluting core identity","founder disengagement (app left on autopilot)"],"lessons_for_input":["Establish a clear, tested monetization path (ad inventory, CPMs, fill rates) before scaling — viral growth without revenue logic burns runway fast.","Design for long-term retention, not just viral acquisition; a single recurring mechanic needs depth or social graph lock-in to survive post-hype churn.","Harden your security posture before going viral — a hack that exposes user data at peak moment can permanently damage trust and stall growth.","Use the location-based community seed deliberately to build a sticky social graph before expanding, so you have a defensible user base when novelty fades.","Avoid feature creep as a substitute for engagement strategy — Yo added links, photos, and location sharing but none of it reversed the retention decline."]}
